english: valley Mil.; *žaṅ-stoṅ* (zhang stong) Ld., *dom-stoṅ* (dom stong) Pur., bare-bottomed, having the bottom bare, vulg.; *mi toṅ-pa* (mi stong pa) W., = *mi kyaṅ* (mi kyang), v. rkyaṅ-pa (rkyang pa); kaṅ-stoṅ (khang stong) a desolate house, as a place suitable for enchantments; fig. *sem toṅ-pa rag* (sems stong pa rag) W. I feel lonely. - stoṅ-pa-nyid (stong pa nyid) शून्यता, emptiness, vacuity, the void, the chief product of the philosophical speculations of the Buddhists, and the aim and end of all their aspirations, v. Köpp. I, 214; Burn. I, 442; 462. (Five synonyms v. Trig. f. 20). stoṅ-zád-la skyél-ba (stong zad la skyel ba) to squander, to waste, use one's life Mil.; stoṅ-saṅ-né (stong sang ne) absolute vacuity, stoṅ-saṅ-né byás-nas (stong sang ne byas nas) making tabula rasa, keeping, retaining nothing whatever Thgy. - stoṅ-gsál (stong gsal) v. gsal-po (gsal po). - Adv. stoṅ-par (stong par) in vain(?) Mil.

1. pf. bstans (bstans) (Dzl.), fut. bstan (bstan) (?), to accompany, *tóṅ-te dó-wa* (stong te 'gro ba) C. to go along with a person; čis kyaṅ mi stóṅs-par čí (cis kyang mi stongs par 'chi) I die without any thing following me Thgy.; more frq. stoṅ-grógs byéd-pa (stong grogs byed pa) ccgp. (also dat.?) to help, to assist a person Mil. - 2. to make empty; to be empty, to become waste or desolate, raṅ-gi gnas stóṅs-śiṅ (rang gi gnas stongs shing) S.g., raṅ-śúl stóṅs-nas (rang shul stongs nas) Mil., your own place becoming desolate; stóṅs-su nyé-bar gyur (stongs su nye bar gyur) it had become nearly empty, was almost spent or exhausted Pth.; mis stóṅs-pai kàṅ-ro (mis stongs pai khang ro) ruins forsaken by men; saṅs-rgyás-kyis stóṅs-pa (sangs rgyas kyis stongs pa) Thgy. the period during which no Buddha appears, a mi-kóm-pa (mi khom pa) v. kóm-pa (khom pa); sa-gźir stóṅs-pa (sa gzhir stongs pa) to level with the ground, to raze, to demolish entirely.

I. the upper, higher, former part of a thing, the upper half opp. to smad (smad); 1. esp. the upper part of the body, resp. sku-stód (sku stod) Pth.; stod-kóg (stod khog) the upper part of a carcase Sch., also stód-po (stod po) Mil.; stod-kyébs (stod khebs) a sort of frill or ruffle of the Lamas; stod-gág (stod gag) doublet of the Lamas, without sleeves; stod-túṅ (stod thung) a short coat, jacket. - 2. the upper or higher part of a country, stód-pa (stod pa) an inhabitant of it, high-lander. - 3. with respect to time: the first part, of the night Dzl., of life Glr., of winter and the like; stód-la (stod la) at the upper part of, above. II. v. stád-pa (stad pa), and stód-pa (stod pa).

1. vb., pf. and fut. bstod (bstod) ('to raise, to exalt', opp. to smád-pa (smad pa)) to praise, commend, laud, bdag-stod-pa (bdag stod pa), W. *raṅ-tod-čè* (rang bstod ce), to praise one's self, *raṅ-tod-čan* (rang bstod can) a self-admirer, self-flatterer; to extol, to glorify, men, gods etc., frq.; stod-(čiṅ) bsṅags-pa (stod cing bsngags pa) id.; stod-tsig (stod tshig) an epithet of praise, a commendable quality. - 2. sbst. praise, eulogy, also *tód-ra* (stod ra) W.; compliments, complimentary phrases e.g. in letters; hymn of praise, also stod-bsṅágs (stod bsngags), stod-dbyáṅs (stod dbyangs), stod-glú (stod glu); stód-pa(r) byéd-pa (stod par byed pa), W. *pul-če* (phul ce), ccd. (the former also c. accus.) to praise, to extol; stod-ós (stod 'os) laudable, commendable, worthy of praise.

1. autumn (more about it v. dus (dus)), ston brgya mtóṅ-bar gyúr čig (ston brgya mthong bar gyur cig) may he live to see a hundred autumns! Lt. - 2. in autumn, during autumn B., frq. - 3. = ston-tóg (ston thog). Comp. stón-ka (ston ka), stón-k'a (ston kha), autumn, *stón-ka-na* (ston ka na), *stón-ka-la* (ston ka la) in autumn, during autumn. - ston-tóg (ston thog) autumnal fruit, harvest, ston-tóg sdú-ba (ston thog sdu ba) (W. also *dóg-če* (sdog ce)) to gather in the produce of the fields, to harvest. - ston-dús (ston dus) harvest-time, autumn, - ston-zlá (ston zla) autumnal month.

I. vb., pf. and fut. bstan (bstan), at the end of a sentence bstan-no (bstan no) (so prob. also in Dzl. 70, 10 the correct reading), W. *(s)tán-če* (stan ce), 1. to show, lam stón-čig (lam ston cig) B., *(s)tán toṅ* (stan tong) W., *ten rog jhe śig* (bstan rogs byed shig) C. show me the way! stón-mkan źig yod (ston mkhan zhig yod) somebody has shown Glr.; bú-mo sgo stón-mkan (bu mo sgo ston mkhan) the girl that has shown the door Mil., mtsán-mkan-la bu stón-pa (mtshan mkhan la bu ston pa) to show the soothsayer a child Dzl.; lus stón-pa (lus ston pa), applied to deities etc.: to show one's self, to appear Dzl.; rdzu-prúl stón-pa (rdzu 'phrul ston pa) to show, to exhibit magic tricks, v. rdzu (rdzu); dmág-pa yín-no źes bstán-te (dmag pa yin no zhes bstan te) 'this is the bridegroom!' with these words showing, i. e. introducing him as the bridegroom <
